# 🎯 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the term "Immersion" in the context of Virtual Reality. Differentiate it from "Presence" with a suitable example.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as marked):

**[1 mark — Definition of Immersion]:**
> *Immersion* is the **objective, technical property** of a VR/AR system that describes the extent to which it can deliver a surrounding sensory environment, isolating the user from the real world and replacing perceptual cues with synthetic ones. It is quantified by engineering specifications such as field-of-view, refresh rate, and tracking fidelity.

**[1 mark — Definition of Presence]:**
> *Presence* is the **subjective psychological state** in which the user consciously experiences "being there" inside the virtual environment despite being physically located in the real world.

**[1 mark — Differentiating Example]:**
> **Example:** A Meta Quest 3 HMD with $110°$ FOV and 120 Hz refresh rate provides *high immersion*. However, if the user is mentally fatigued or the content is uninteresting, they may *not feel present* in the virtual scene — immersion is the system's *capability*, while presence is the user's *experience*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "Immersion is the feeling of being there" — this is the definition of **Presence**, not Immersion. Marker's will deduct **1 full mark** for this swap. Always attribute Immersion to the **system** and Presence to the **user's mind**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**State and briefly explain Burdea's "3I's Model" of Immersive Virtual Reality.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

**[1 mark — Formula statement]:**

$$\text{Immersive VR} = I^3 = \text{Immersion} + \text{Interaction} + \text{Information (Imagination)}$$

**[1 mark — Each I explained]:**
- **Immersion** — Sensory envelopment provided by the hardware (HMD, audio, haptics).
- **Interaction** — Real-time, bidirectional manipulation between user and environment.
- **Imagination/Information** — Cognitive content, narrative, and mental model that gives meaning to the experience.

**[1 mark — Why all three are necessary]:**
> If any of the three is missing, the system is *not* truly immersive. A high-FOV HMD with no interaction is a *movie*; an interactive system with no immersion is a *game on a monitor*; an immersive + interactive system with no meaningful content is *pointless immersion*.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Milgram–Kishino Reality–Virtuality Continuum** with a neat diagram. Discuss how Augmented Reality (AR) and Augmented Virtuality (AV) differ in terms of user perception and technical implementation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **causes and measurement of Presence** in VR systems. Compare any two standardized presence questionnaires with their sub-scales. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**[1 mark — Definition + 1 mark — Diagram description]:**
> The **Reality–Virtuality Continuum**, proposed by Milgram and Kishino (1994), is a taxonomy that classifies display environments on a continuous spectrum between the *Real Environment* (RE) at one extreme and a fully *Virtual Environment* (VE) at the other. The intermediate region is termed **Mixed Reality (MR)**, which comprises both **Augmented Reality (AR)** and **Augmented Virtuality (AV)**.

```
RE ◄──── AR ──── AV ────► VE
                │
              MR
```

**[2 marks — AR vs AV technical]:**
- **AR** predominantly retains the real-world view, with *digital content overlaid* on top. Implementation uses *optical see-through HMDs* (e.g., HoloLens, Magic Leap) or *video see-through* (e.g., smartphone ARKit/ARCore). The user sees the real environment *directly* through transparent combiners, with computer graphics *additively* rendered on a partially transmissive surface.
- **AV** is predominantly virtual but embeds *real-world captured elements* (e.g., live video textures of real objects) into the synthetic world. Implementation typically uses *video see-through HMDs* or *CAVE systems* with composited camera feeds. The user sees a virtual scene where selected real elements are dynamically injected.

**[2 marks — Perceptual differences]:**
- In AR, the *real world is the anchor*; virtual objects are *guests*. Depth perception uses *real-world cues* (occlusion, shadows, lighting). The user understands the virtual content as overlaid information.
- In AV, the *virtual world is the anchor*; real-world objects are *guests*. Depth perception is *predominantly synthetic*, with real objects requiring calibration to fit the virtual lighting model.

**[1 mark — Use-case examples]:**
- AR: Surgical guidance overlays on a patient; Pokémon GO; fighter-pilot HUD.
- AV: Virtual collaboration rooms with live 360° camera feeds of remote participants; digital-twin inspection of a real engine inside a virtual maintenance bay.

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**[2 marks — Definition + causes]:**
> Presence is the *subjective feeling of "being there"* in a virtual environment. Its three principal causes are: (i) **Sensorimotor Contingencies** — valid, low-latency mappings between body movement and sensory feedback; (ii) **Cognitive Factors** — user's prior knowledge, expectations, and *immersion tendency* (a stable personality trait); (iii) **Technological Fidelity** — display, audio, and tracking quality. Of these, sensorimotor contingencies are the **strongest predictor** (Slater & Wilbur, 1997).

**[2.5 marks — Witmer–Singer Presence Questionnaire]:**
> The **Witmer–Singer Presence Questionnaire (WSPQ)** has 32 items scored on a 1–7 Likert scale, aggregated into four sub-scales:
> - **Involvement** — attentional engagement with the environment.
> - **Sensory Fidelity** — multimodal richness.
> - **Adaptation/Immersion** — ease of interaction.
> - **Interface Quality** — absence of distracting hardware effects.
> 
> Total possible score: $32 \times 7 = 224$.

**[2.5 marks — Igroup Presence Questionnaire (IPQ)]:**
> The **igroup Presence Questionnaire (IPQ)** uses 14 items across three sub-scales and one general item:
> - **Spatial Presence (SP)** — 5 items (feeling of being in the VE).
> - **Involvement (INV)** — 4 items (attention capture).
> - **Experienced Realism (REAL)** — 4 items (believability).
> - **General Presence (GP)** — 1 single-item rating on a 0–6 scale.
> 
> Each sub-scale is averaged independently; the General item is reported separately.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often describe *only one* questionnaire. KTU valuation explicitly awards **2.5 marks** for each correct comparative discussion. If you list both but do not compare sub-scales, expect a **1-mark deduction**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the **Field of View (FOV)** for a Head-Mounted Display with focal length $f = 40 \text{ mm}$ and a square micro-display of side $s = 50 \text{ mm}$. Comment on whether the FOV is sufficient to fill the human visual field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a labelled diagram, explain **Bryson's Levels of Immersion**. Discuss the role of **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** in interaction fidelity for each level. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**[Step 1 — Geometry Setup, 1 mark]:**
> A thin lens of focal length $f$ focuses a micro-display at optical infinity for the relaxed eye. The half-angle subtended by the display at the eye-pupil is given by:

$$\tan(\theta_{\text{half}}) = \frac{s/2}{f}$$

**[Step 2 — Substitute numerical values, 1 mark]:**
$$\tan(\theta_{\text{half}}) = \frac{25 \text{ mm}}{40 \text{ mm}} = 0.625$$

**[Step 3 — Evaluate the half-angle, 1 mark]:**
$$\theta_{\text{half}} = \arctan(0.625) = 32.005^{\circ}$$

**[Step 4 — Total FOV, 1 mark]:**
$$\boxed{\text{FOV}_{\text{side}} = 2 \cdot 32.005^{\circ} = 64.01^{\circ}}$$

**[Step 5 — Discussion of sufficiency, 3 marks]:**
> The **human binocular FOV** is approximately $200°$ horizontal × $135°$ vertical. A square-display HMD provides equal horizontal and vertical FOV of $\approx 64°$. This is **far below** the natural human field of view and falls into **Level-1 to Level-2** of Bryson's taxonomy.
> 
> **Implications:**
> - The user sees a "porthole" view — peripheral vision is *not* filled.
> - Presence is reduced because peripheral cues (motion in side vision) are absent.
> - Modern HMDs (e.g., Pimax $8K$ X, Meta Quest Pro) address this by using **larger displays** or **pancake lenses** with shorter focal lengths ($f = 20–25 \text{ mm}$) to achieve $110°$–$200°$ FOV.
> 
> **Conclusion:** The computed $64°$ FOV is **insufficient** for full human visual field filling and is classified as a low-immersion display.

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**[4 marks — Diagram + Levels explanation]:**
> Steve Bryson (1995) classified immersive systems into **five levels** based on the degree of sensory enclosure:

| Level | Name | Example | FOV | Stereo |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Desktop Monitor | Standard PC | ~40° | Mono |
| 1 | Fish-Tank VR | Monitor + shutter glasses | ~40° | Active |
| 2 | Wide-Field Immersive | Curved screen, CAVE | 120°–180° | Multi |
| 3 | Head-Mounted Display | Quest, Vive | 90°–210° | Passive |
| 4 | Fully Immersive | CAVE + HMD + haptics | 360° | Multi-user |

```
L0 → L1 → L2 → L3 → L4
(Monitor)  (FishTank)  (WideField)  (HMD)  (Full Immersion)
```

**[3 marks — DOF in interaction fidelity]:**
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** refer to the number of independent positional and rotational axes the system tracks.
> 
> - **3-DOF systems** track *orientation only* — yaw (left/right), pitch (up/down), roll (tilt). Example: Google Cardboard. The user *cannot physically walk* in the scene; their position is anchored. This severely limits natural interaction.
> - **6-DOF systems** track *position + orientation* — adding $x, y, z$ translation. Example: Meta Quest 3. The user can physically walk, crouch, and lean — enabling natural exploration and manipulation.
> 
> **Per-level DOF mapping:**
> - **L0–L1:** Usually 0–3 DOF (no positional tracking).
> - **L2:** 6-DOF for head + 6-DOF for wand controllers.
> - **L3–L4:** Full 6-DOF head + 6-DOF hand + (optional) eye tracking → up to **12+ DOF** with multiple tracked objects.
> 
> Higher DOF → higher **interaction fidelity** → greater sense of agency → enhanced presence.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students commonly confuse **DOF in tracking** (the system's tracked axes) with **DOF in robotics** (joint articulations). In AR/VR KTU context, **DOF always refers to the tracked motion of the user's body/head/controllers in 3D space**, not robotic joint angles.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wise Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP-5 Common Mark-Deduction Traps in this Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Swapping definitions:** Writing "Immersion is the *feeling* of being there" (this is Presence). Loses **1–2 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Conflating FOV types:** Not distinguishing *horizontal*, *vertical*, and *diagonal* FOV in numerical problems. Always specify. Loses **1 mark**.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring units:** Writing FOV in radians when KTU expects degrees (or vice-versa). Always state units. Loses **0.5–1 mark**.
> 
> 4. **Treating Presence as binary:** Presence is a *spectrum*, not present/absent. Use IPQ/SPQ scale (0–6 or 1–7). Loses **1 mark**.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in "explain with diagram" questions:** A missing diagram forfeits **1–2 marks** even if text is perfect. Always include at least a labelled block-diagram or ASCII sketch.
