# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an HMD. List its key components. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Model Answer:**
A Head-Mounted Display (HMD) is a wearable display device that projects stereoscopic images directly into the user's eyes, replacing the real-world view with virtual content.
**Key Components:**
1. **Display panels** (LCD/OLED/Micro-OLED) — one per eye.
2. **Optical lenses** (Fresnel/Pancake/Aspheric) for focus and FOV control.
3. **IMU** — Gyroscope + Accelerometer + Magnetometer for orientation tracking.
4. **Positional tracking sensors** — cameras (inside-out) or photodiodes (outside-in).
5. **Headstrap and housing** — ergonomic mounting.
6. **Audio system** — integrated headphones or 3D spatial audio drivers.
7. **SoC/processor** — for standalone units (e.g., Snapdragon XR2).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write *"HMD has two screens"* without mentioning the **stereoscopic** aspect (one per eye, with **parallax**). This loses **1 mark**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between 3DOF and 6DOF in motion controllers. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 3DOF | 6DOF |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | 3 Degrees of Freedom | 6 Degrees of Freedom |
| Axes Tracked | Roll, Pitch, Yaw only | Roll, Pitch, Yaw + X, Y, Z |
| Translation | No | Yes |
| Tracking | IMU only (orientation) | IMU + Cameras / Lighthouses |
| Example | Samsung Gear VR remote | Meta Quest Touch Plus |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Immersion | Limited | High |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a modern VR HMD with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the IMU, the display panels, and the optical lens system in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks)** What is motion-to-photon latency? Derive the frame budget for a 90 Hz VR system. Why is keeping this latency under **20 ms** critical?

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**1. Architecture Overview** (2 marks)
A modern VR HMD consists of:
- **Front enclosure** — houses displays and lenses.
- **Rear strap** — provides ergonomic fit.
- **Top strap** — distributes weight.
- **Onboard SoC** (standalone) or **tethered cable** (PC-connected).
- **Audio strap** with integrated speakers.

**2. Display Panel Role** (2 marks)
- Two micro-displays (one per eye) render left and right images.
- Technologies: **LCD** (cheap, low contrast), **OLED** (high contrast, deep blacks), **Micro-OLED** (Apple Vision Pro, highest PPD).
- Resolution: 1440×1600 to 3660×3200 per eye.
- Refresh rate: 72 Hz, 90 Hz, 120 Hz, or 144 Hz.

**3. Optical Lens Role** (2 marks)
- Lenses focus close display into the eye's far point.
- **Fresnel lenses** — cheap, light, but show "god rays."
- **Pancake lenses** — folded optics, slimmer HMDs, used in Quest 3 and Apple Vision Pro.
- **Aspheric lenses** — best clarity, used in Pimax.
- Lenses determine **FOV** and **eye relief** (15–24 mm).

**4. IMU Role** (1 mark)
- Captures angular velocity (gyro), linear acceleration (accelerometer), and heading (magnetometer) at 1000 Hz.
- Sensor fusion (Madgwick/Mahony filter) gives orientation quaternion.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**1. Definition of Motion-to-Photon Latency** (2 marks)
It is the time elapsed from a user's physical head movement to the moment the corresponding new pixel becomes visible on the display.

$$ L = T_\text{sense} + T_\text{render} + T_\text{warp} + T_\text{display} $$

**2. Frame Budget Derivation for 90 Hz** (3 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_\text{frame} &= \frac{1}{90}\ \text{s} \\
&= 11.11\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
11.11 &= T_\text{IMU} + T_\text{render} + T_\text{warp} + T_\text{display} \\
11.11 &= 1.0 + 8.0 + 0.5 + 1.6\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Why ≤ 20 ms is Critical** (2 marks)
- The human vestibular system detects motion change in **20–25 ms**.
- Latency above 20 ms causes **vection–vestibular mismatch**, leading to **simulator sickness**, nausea, and disorientation.
- Industry rule: latency < 20 ms ensures the brain accepts the virtual motion as real.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "lower latency is better" without quoting the **20 ms threshold** and linking it to the **vestibular system**. This loses **2 marks**.

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, explain the working principle of an omnidirectional VR treadmill. Compare sliding-platform and belt-array designs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain outside-in and inside-out tracking. Show how a Lighthouse basestation computes the position of a controller using IR laser sweeps.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**1. Working Principle** (3 marks)
An ODT captures the user's foot velocity $\vec{v}_\text{foot}$ in the horizontal plane and maps it to the avatar's walking direction in VR. The user remains physically centered while traversing unlimited virtual distances.

**2. Sliding-Platform (Virtuix Omni style)** (2 marks)
- Concave dish with smooth surface.
- User wears low-friction shoes ($\mu \approx 0.05$).
- Four force sensors at dish base compute foot slip.
- Virtual spring force at center keeps user within active zone.

**3. Belt-Array (Kat Walk style)** (2 marks)
- Multiple narrow motorized belts arranged radially.
- Belts spin in coordination to "carry" the user's foot back to the center as they step.
- $v_\text{belt} = -v_\text{user}$ for self-centering.
- More natural gait, no slippery shoes required.

**Comparison Table:** (Already given in Section 3.4.3 — key bullets suffice for 14-mark answer.)

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**1. Outside-in Tracking** (2 marks)
External basestations/cameras observe the device.
- **Lighthouse v2 (Valve):** Two rotating IR laser fans sweep the room at 100 Hz each (X and Y planes).
- Photodiodes on the controller measure arrival time → angle to base.
- Position triangulated using the two angles from two basestations.

**2. Inside-out Tracking** (2 marks)
- Cameras on the HMD observe **IR LEDs** (or visible markers) on the controller.
- Onboard SLAM algorithm computes controller pose relative to HMD.

**3. Lighthouse Position Math** (3 marks)
A photodiode at controller position $(x_c, y_c, z_c)$ measures the IR laser sweep at time $t$.

$$
\theta = \omega_\text{lens} \cdot t
$$

where $\omega_\text{lens}$ is the motor's angular speed (e.g., 50 rad/s).
The bearing angle to the basestation is:

$$
\tan(\phi) = \frac{y_c - y_b}{x_c - x_b}
$$

With two basestations, intersecting the two bearing lines yields $(x_c, y_c)$. The $z$ axis is recovered from the **fixed-axis laser** and the second sweeping axis. Sub-millimeter accuracy is achieved.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** In Lighthouse-based math, students often confuse the **rotating laser** (gives angle) with the **fixed laser** (gives timing reference). This loses **2 marks**. Always mention **two sweeps per basestation** for full 6DOF.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **HMD** = wearable stereoscopic display + tracking. Three classes: **Tethered, Standalone, Mobile-based**.
- **FOV** matters: 90°–130° typical. Narrow FOV breaks presence.
- **PPD ≥ 15** eliminates screen-door effect; **PPD ≥ 30** is retina-level (Apple Vision Pro).
- **Refresh rate** must be ≥ 90 Hz to avoid flicker sickness; 120 Hz is the 2024 standard.
- **Motion-to-Photon Latency ≤ 20 ms** — industry hard rule. Breakdown: **IMU + Render + Warp + Display**.
- **IMU** = Gyro + Accelerometer + Magnetometer. Fused at 1000 Hz via Madgwick/Mahony/Kalman filter.
- **Tracking types:** **Outside-in** (Lighthouse — sub-mm accuracy) vs **Inside-out** (mobile, no setup).
- **3DOF** = rotation only. **6DOF** = rotation + translation. Always prefer 6DOF for full immersion.
- **Motion Controller** standard parts: 2× buttons, 1× trigger, 1× thumbstick, 1× grip button, haptic LRA, IMU, IR LEDs.
- **ODT types:** Sliding-dish (Virtuix), Belt-array (Kat Walk), Sphere-shoes (Cyberith).
- **Friction formula:** $F_f = \mu m g$ — low $\mu$ (≈ 0.05) protects knees and feels natural.
- **Treadmill center-restoring force:** $\vec{F}_\text{restore} = -k(\vec{r} - \vec{r}_\text{center})$, with $k \approx 50$ N/m.
- **Lighthouse principle:** Two IR laser fans sweep X and Y at 100 Hz each. Photo-sensor timing → bearing angle.
- **Key products to remember for KTU viva:** Quest 3, Valve Index, PSVR2, Apple Vision Pro, HTC Vive Pro 2, Virtuix Omni, Kat Walk.
- **Common examiner traps:** (1) Forgetting **stereoscopic** in HMD definition, (2) quoting "low latency" without the **20 ms** value, (3) confusing **DOF count** (3 vs 6), (4) skipping **center-restoring force** in treadmill answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Architectures for AR and VR Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT753 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software Architectures for AR and VR Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Architecture for AR/VR Systems** is the organized, layered, and modular blueprint of software components, data flows, rendering pipelines, sensor abstractions, and runtime services that collectively enable real-time synthesis, presentation, and interaction within an Augmented Reality (AR) or Virtual Reality (VR) experience.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is the **structural skeleton** that binds together the rendering engine, scene graph, tracking module, sensor fusion layer, input/output handlers, and the display compositor. It defines **how data moves from physical sensors → logical scene representation → rendered frames → the user's eyes**, all under strict real-time constraints (latency ≤ **20 ms** for VR, ≤ **5 ms** for AR overlay).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus:**
> The KTU syllabus explicitly asks students to understand *pipeline architectures, layered architectures, distributed architectures, and reference models* such as the **VR Rendering Pipeline (VRRP)** and the **AR Compositor Pipeline (ARCP)**. Marks are frequently awarded in Part B for diagrams of these pipelines.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Movie Production Studio

Imagine you are producing a live, interactive 3D movie inside a small box that the user wears on their head. You have:

| Movie Studio Role | AR/VR Software Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Script & Storyboard | **Scene Graph** — the digital description of objects |
| Director | **Rendering Engine** — orchestrates how every frame looks |
| Cameraman | **Tracking System** — reports head/eye movement |
| Lighting Crew | **Illumination & Shading Module** |
| Stage Manager | **Event / Input Handler** — accepts controller, gesture, voice |
| Projectionist | **Display Compositor** — sends pixels to HMD screens |
| Producer | **Application Framework / Game Engine** (Unity, Unreal) |

Just as a movie studio has **departments that must coordinate with millisecond precision** (a delay of even one frame breaks the illusion), an AR/VR software architecture has **strict, time-bounded modules** that must work together. The architecture is the **org chart** of that studio.

---

## 1.3 Why a Specialized Architecture? — The Real-Time Mandate

Unlike conventional desktop software, AR/VR systems must respect three physics-imposed rules:

1. **Motion-to-Photon Latency (MTP)** — the time between a user's physical head motion and the corresponding updated pixel reaching the retina. Anything beyond **20 ms** causes *cybersickness* (VR sickness / simulator sickness).
2. **Sustained Frame Rate** — typically **90 Hz, 120 Hz**, or even **144 Hz** depending on the HMD.
3. **Spatial Coherence** — virtual objects must remain correctly registered with the real world (AR) or with the user's vestibular sense (VR).

A *generic* software architecture (e.g., a typical web app) cannot guarantee these. Hence AR/VR demands **specialized, real-time-capable, multi-threaded, pipelined architectures**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Architectures (KTU-Canonical)

KTU expects familiarity with **five architectural styles**:

1. **Monolithic Game-Engine Architecture** — Unity/Unreal style, single large runtime.
2. **Layered / Tiered Architecture** — Application → Framework → OS Abstraction → Hardware Driver.
3. **Pipeline Architecture** — Streaming, parallel, time-sliced stages (most common in HMDs).
4. **Distributed / Client-Server Architecture** — Cloud VR, multi-user VR, edge-rendered VR.
5. **Service-Oriented / Microservices Architecture** — Modular AR cloud services (e.g., ARKit/ARCore services).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant / Metric You MUST Memorize for KTU:**
> - **Target VR refresh rate: 90 Hz** (frame budget ≈ **11.11 ms**)
> - **Target AR overlay latency: ≤ 5 ms** post-sensor read
> - **Interpupillary Distance (IPD): ~63 mm** (adult human average)
> - **Acceptable FOV for consumer VR: 90° – 120°**
> - **Pixel Per Degree (PPD) benchmark: ≥ 15 PPD** for retinal resolution (vision acuity 1.0)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Architectural Styles

### A. Monolithic Game-Engine Architecture

A single, large runtime binary that contains the rendering engine, physics engine, audio engine, scripting host (e.g., C#/Lua), asset manager, and input subsystem. Examples: **Unity Runtime, Unreal Engine runtime**.

**Pros:** Tight integration, low overhead, easy debugging.
**Cons:** Hard to scale, heavy memory footprint (~2–4 GB), single point of failure.

### B. Layered Architecture

A clean, modular separation into four tiers:

| Layer | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | User logic, gameplay, business rules | C#/C++ scripts, Unity Components |
| **Framework Layer** | Scene graph, asset pipeline, scripting | Unity Editor, Unreal Blueprints |
| **OS Abstraction Layer** | Threading, file I/O, networking | POSIX / Win32 wrappers |
| **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** | Direct GPU/CPU/Driver calls | Vulkan, OpenXR, OpenGL ES |

> [!NOTE]
> This is the architecture most often **drawn in KTU 2-mark definition questions**.

### C. Pipeline (Streaming / Parallel-Stage) Architecture

The dominant architecture inside the HMD and rendering servers. Stages execute in **parallel** with **double or triple buffering**:

```
Sensor Read → Sensor Fusion → Pose Prediction → View Cull → Render → Distortion Correction → Display Scan-out
```

Each stage has a **fixed time budget**. If one stage overruns, the frame is dropped (a *jank* event). This is essentially a **hardware pipeline mapped to software**.

### D. Distributed / Client-Server Architecture

For Cloud VR / Wireless VR (e.g., NVIDIA CloudXR, Google Stadia VR, SteamVR streaming). The **server** performs heavy rendering; the **client (HMD)** only does pose prediction, decompression, and distortion correction.

**Key trade-off:** Server has unlimited GPU power but introduces **network latency**. To meet the 20 ms MTP budget, the *round-trip time (RTT)* must stay below **~10 ms**, requiring 5G/edge computing.

### E. Service-Oriented / Microservices Architecture

The AR Cloud paradigm. Independent services for: **mapping, anchoring, lighting estimation, occlusion, relocalization, shared state, social presence, content delivery**. These communicate via REST/gRPC. Examples: **ARCore Cloud Anchors, Azure Spatial Anchors, Niantic Lightship**.

---

## 2.2 Core Components of any AR/VR Software Architecture

Regardless of style, every AR/VR runtime contains the following **logical components**:

1. **Sensor Manager** — abstracts IMU, cameras, depth sensors, eye trackers.
2. **Sensor Fusion & Pose Estimation Module** — combines IMU (high-rate) with optical tracking (low-rate but accurate) using **Kalman Filter** or **complementary filter**.
3. **Scene Graph** — hierarchical representation of 3D objects, transforms, lights.
4. **Rendering Engine** — issues draw calls via **Vulkan / OpenGL / DirectX 12**.
5. **Compositor** — final image assembly, alpha blending for AR overlays.
6. **Distortion / Lens Correction Module** — pre-distorts the image to compensate for HMD lens pincushion distortion.
7. **Input Subsystem** — controllers, hand tracking, gaze, voice.
8. **Audio Engine** — spatialized 3D audio (HRTF-based).
9. **Networking Module** — for multiplayer VR / cloud anchors.
10. **Application Lifecycle Manager** — load/save, session, power management.

---

## 2.3 The VR Rendering Pipeline (VRRP) — Stage-by-Stage

| Stage | Typical Time Budget | Hardware Responsible |
|---|---|---|
| 1. IMU Sampling | < 1 ms | Dedicated IMU MCU |
| 2. Pose Prediction | 1–2 ms | Application CPU |
| 3. View Frustum Culling | 1 ms | CPU |
| 4. Draw Call Submission | 2–3 ms | CPU → GPU command buffer |
| 5. Vertex / Fragment Shading | 4–6 ms | GPU |
| 6. Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW) | 1 ms | GPU (always-on fallback) |
| 7. Lens Distortion & Scan-out | 1–2 ms | Display controller |
| **Total MTP** | **~11 ms @ 90 Hz** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List the stages of the VR rendering pipeline."* Memorize the table above.

---

## 2.4 The AR Compositor Pipeline (ARCP)

AR adds an extra reality-capture and merging step:

1. **RGB-Depth Capture** (one or more cameras)
2. **SLAM / Visual-Inertial Odometry** (VIO)
3. **Plane Detection / Mesh Reconstruction**
4. **Anchor Management** (track feature points)
5. **Virtual Object Rendering**
6. **Occlusion Handling** (Depth buffer with real scene)
7. **Lighting Estimation** (real-world directional light)
8. **Alpha Compositing** onto the camera feed
9. **Display Output**

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet — Memorize This Table.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{fps} = \dfrac{1}{t_{frame}}$ | Frame rate from frame time | Latency questions |
| 2 | $t_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f_{fps}}$ | Frame budget | Compute budget for 90 Hz / 120 Hz |
| 3 | $L_{MTP} = t_{sense} + t_{fusion} + t_{cull} + t_{render} + t_{composite} + t_{display}$ | Motion-to-photon latency | Most critical VR metric |
| 4 | $L_{MTPD} = 2 \cdot t_{frame}$ | Maximum tolerable delay before sickness | Practical latency budget |
| 5 | $L_{RTT} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{d_{km}}{v_{fiber}}$ | Network round-trip (speed of light in fiber, ~ 200,000 km/s) | Cloud VR streaming |
| 6 | $\theta_{FOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d_{sensor}}{2 \cdot f}\right)$ | Field of view from focal length | Optics |
| 7 | $PPD = \dfrac{H_{pixels}}{\theta_{FOV,H}}$ | Pixels per degree (visual acuity) | Display resolution |
| 8 | $P_{pitch} = P_{eye} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta t_{motion}}{\Delta t_{render}}$ | Required predictive pose shifts | ATW / prediction |
| 9 | $D_{bandwidth} = W \cdot H \cdot f_{fps} \cdot b_{pp} \cdot 3$ | Video bandwidth (W×H resolution, f fps, b bits/pixel, 3 for stereo+chrom) | Cloud VR streaming |
| 10 | $R_{tracking} = f_{IMU} \cdot \Delta t$ | Tracking samples per render frame | Sensor fusion |

**Boundary Conditions / Acceptable Ranges (must memorize):**

- VR: $L_{MTP} \le 20 \text{ ms}$
- AR: $L_{MTP} \le 5 \text{ ms}$ (overlay add-on)
- VR: $f_{fps} \ge 90 \text{ Hz}$ → $t_{frame} \le 11.11 \text{ ms}$
- VR: $f_{fps} \ge 120 \text{ Hz}$ → $t_{frame} \le 8.33 \text{ ms}$
- Human retina (20/20 vision): **PPD ≥ 60** for *true* retinal resolution, **PPD ≥ 15** for "good enough".

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This architectural knowledge directly powers:

- **Cloud XR / 5G XR** — by partitioning rendering between edge server and HMD (Section 2.1 D).
- **Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro, Microsoft HoloLens** — all use pipeline architectures with ATW (Section 2.3).
- **Industrial Digital Twins** — distributed AR microservices for factory workers.
- **Surgical AR (e.g., AccuVein, Medivis)** — strict latency for safety-critical overlay.
- **Autonomous Driving AR HUDs** — extremely low MTP requirement (~5 ms).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section performs **exhaustive step-by-step derivations** of every formula in the cheat sheet above, with no skips.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frame Budget from Frame Rate

**Given:** A VR headset operates at a refresh rate of $f_{fps} = 120 \text{ Hz}$.
**Find:** The per-frame time budget $t_{frame}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{frame} &= \frac{1}{f_{fps}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{120 \text{ s}^{-1}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.00833\ldots \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 8.33 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every stage of the VR pipeline combined must finish in **8.33 ms**, leaving no slack. If rendering exceeds this budget, the next vsync is missed → *stutter / dropped frame*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motion-to-Photon Latency Breakdown

**Given:** A pipeline with the following stage times for a typical 90 Hz VR system:

| Stage | Symbol | Time |
|---|---|---|
| IMU Sampling | $t_{sense}$ | 1 ms |
| Pose Fusion & Prediction | $t_{fusion}$ | 1 ms |
| View Culling & Draw Submission | $t_{cull}$ | 1 ms |
| GPU Rendering (vertex + fragment) | $t_{render}$ | 5 ms |
| Timewarp + Distortion | $t_{composite}$ | 1 ms |
| Display Scan-out | $t_{display}$ | 2 ms |

**Find:** Total $L_{MTP}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{MTP} &= t_{sense} + t_{fusion} + t_{cull} + t_{render} + t_{composite} + t_{display} \\[4pt]
&= 1 + 1 + 1 + 5 + 1 + 2 \\[4pt]
&= 11 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $L_{MTP} = 11 \text{ ms}$, which is *under* the 20 ms cybersickness threshold, leaving a **9 ms safety margin**. KTU marks full credit for showing the summation explicitly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Network Round-Trip for Cloud VR (Light in Fiber)

**Given:** A cloud-rendered VR stream from an edge server at distance $d = 50 \text{ km}$ via fiber optic cable. Speed of light in fiber ≈ $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.
**Find:** Round-trip latency $L_{RTT}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{fiber} &= 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
d_{m} &= 50 \times 10^{3} \text{ m} \\[4pt]
t_{one\text{-}way} &= \frac{d_m}{v_{fiber}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 10^{8}} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 0.25 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
L_{RTT} &= 2 \cdot t_{one\text{-}way} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot 0.25 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** At 50 km, network adds only **0.5 ms** — comfortably within budget. At **1000 km**, it would be **10 ms** — already half the MTP budget, requiring aggressive prediction and edge caching.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Required Bandwidth for Cloud VR Streaming

**Given:** Stereoscopic (left+right) $1832 \times 1920$ per eye, 90 Hz, 8 bits per channel, 3 color channels.
**Find:** Required bandwidth in Gbps (uncompressed).

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 1832 \text{ px}, \quad H = 1920 \text{ px} \\[4pt]
f_{fps} &= 90 \text{ Hz}, \quad b_{pp} = 8 \text{ bits/channel}, \quad ch = 3 \\[4pt]
\text{per eye, per frame bits} &= W \cdot H \cdot b_{pp} \cdot ch \\[4pt]
&= 1832 \times 1920 \times 8 \times 3 \\[4pt]
&= 84,418,560 \text{ bits/frame (per eye)} \\[4pt]
\text{stereo bits per frame} &= 2 \times 84,418,560 \\[4pt]
&= 168,837,120 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
\text{bits per second} &= 168,837,120 \times 90 \\[4pt]
&= 15,195,340,800 \text{ bits/s} \\[4pt]
D_{bandwidth} &\approx 15.2 \text{ Gbps (uncompressed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** Uncompressed stereo VR at 90 Hz demands **~15 Gbps** — far exceeding Wi-Fi 6 (~9.6 Gbps) and current 5G. Hence **heavy compression (H.264/H.265/AV1)** and **foveated streaming** are essential.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Pixel Per Degree from HMD Specs

**Given:** HMD resolution per eye $2160 \times 2160$, horizontal FOV $\theta_{FOV,H} = 106°$.
**Find:** $PPD$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPD &= \frac{H_{pixels}}{\theta_{FOV,H}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2160}{106} \\[4pt]
&\approx 20.4 \text{ PPD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** Human 20/20 vision ≈ **60 PPD**; the human fovea peak ≈ **30 PPD**. Therefore this HMD is *acceptable* but not retinal. Modern headsets (Vision Pro 2, Quest Pro 3) target **25–30 PPD**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Field of View from Sensor Size and Focal Length

**Given:** Sensor width $d_{sensor} = 11.2 \text{ mm}$, focal length $f = 14 \text{ mm}$.
**Find:** Horizontal FOV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{FOV} &= 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_{sensor}}{2 \cdot f}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{11.2}{2 \times 14}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{11.2}{28}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \arctan(0.4) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 21.80° \\[4pt]
&\approx 43.6°
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This narrow FOV suits a *lens*, not a wide HMD. To achieve a 110° HMD, you need shorter focal length (~6–8 mm) or wider sensor.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Architecture Selection

| Requirement | Best Architecture | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single-user standalone VR headset | **Monolithic Engine** | Tight integration, no network dependency |
| Surgical AR with millimeter precision | **Pipeline + ATW** | Deterministic, lowest MTP |
| 50-player social VR | **Distributed Client-Server** | Authoritative state replication |
| Factory AR with shared annotations | **Microservices (AR Cloud)** | Persistent spatial anchors |
| Mobile AR (phone) | **Layered + Pipeline** | Battery + thermal constraints |
| Cloud-rendered VR over 5G | **Distributed Edge** | Offload GPU; meet MTP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VR System Software Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensors: IMU + Cameras + Eye Tracker] --> B[Sensor Fusion and Pose Estimator]
    B --> C[Pose Predictor ATW]
    C --> D[Scene Graph Manager]
    D --> E[View Frustum Culler]
    E --> F[Rendering Engine OpenGL Vulkan DX12]
    F --> G[Asynchronous Timewarp Compositor]
    G --> H[Lens Distortion and Chromatic Aberration Corrector]
    H --> I[HMD Display Panels 90Hz 120Hz]
    J[Input: Controllers Hand Tracking Voice] --> K[Input Event Manager]
    K --> D
    L[Audio Engine HRTF Spatial] --> I
    M[Network Module Multiplayer] --> D
    N[App Lifecycle Manager] --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:** Data flows from physical sensors on the left through a strict real-time pipeline to the display panels on the right. The **Pose Predictor (ATW)** is the latency-mitigation hero — it runs an extra late-stage warp to ensure pixels always match the *latest* head pose even if the main render missed a frame.

---

## 4.2 AR System Software Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[RGB Camera Left] --> VIO[Visual Inertial Odometry SLAM]
    S2[RGB Camera Right] --> VIO
    S3[Depth Sensor ToF Structured Light] --> VIO
    S4[IMU] --> VIO
    VIO --> P1[Plane Detection]
    VIO --> P2[Mesh Reconstruction]
    VIO --> P3[Relocalization]
    P1 --> ANCHOR[Anchor Manager]
    P2 --> ANCHOR
    P3 --> ANCHOR
    ANCHOR --> SCG[Scene Graph]
    LE[Lighting Estimation Module] --> SCG
    SCG --> RE[AR Rendering Engine]
    OCC[Occlusion Mask Generator] --> RE
    RE --> COMP[Alpha Compositor]
    S1 --> COMP
    COMP --> DSP[HUD or HMD Display]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The AR pipeline has a **dual reality stream** — the real camera feed goes directly to the compositor, while virtual objects are rendered separately and *alpha-blended* on top. Occlusion is computed by comparing virtual depth against the real depth buffer.

---

## 4.3 Distributed (Cloud VR) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HMD[Client HMD Pose Prediction Decompression] -- Pose Data 90Hz --> EDGE[Edge Cloud Server]
    EDGE -- Rendered Frames Encoded 60fps --> HMD
    EDGE --> SRV[Central Game Server]
    SRV --> DB[(Spatial Anchor DB)]
    SRV --> AN[Analytics and Telemetry]
    HMD --> CR[CDN Content Delivery]
    CR --> HMD
```

**Reading the diagram:** The HMD only sends its *pose* (a few hundred bytes), and receives a *compressed rendered frame*. This is exactly how **NVIDIA CloudXR, Google Immersive Stream for XR**, and **Amazon Nimble Studio** operate.

---

## 4.4 Microservices AR Architecture (AR Cloud)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[AR Application Client SDK] --> GW[API Gateway gRPC REST]
    GW --> MS1[Mapping Service]
    GW --> MS2[Anchor Persistence Service]
    GW --> MS3[Lighting Estimation Service]
    GW --> MS4[Occlusion Service]
    GW --> MS5[Relocalization Service]
    GW --> MS6[Social Presence Service]
    MS2 --> DB1[(Spatial DB)]
    MS1 --> DB1
    MS4 --> DB2[(Mesh Cache)]
    MS6 --> MQ[Pub Sub: Kafka or Redis]
    APP <--> MQ
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each AR feature is a *separate microservice* that scales independently. The application SDK orchestrates calls through an API gateway. This is the blueprint behind **Niantic Lightship, Azure Spatial Anchors, ARCore Cloud Anchors**.

---

## 4.5 Layered Architecture (Cross-Cutting View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer: Game Logic UI Scripts]
    L2[Framework Layer: Scene Graph Asset Pipeline Scripting Host]
    L3[Middleware Layer: Physics Audio XR Plugin OpenXR]
    L4[OS Abstraction Layer: Threads File IO Network]
    L5[Hardware Abstraction Layer: GPU Driver Vulkan Display Controller]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

**Reading the diagram:** Information flows *downward* on call (e.g., draw call) and *upward* on data (e.g., sensor read). The HAL layer isolates hardware changes from the application — a key KTU point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Motion-to-Photon Latency" and state the maximum acceptable value for a comfortable VR experience.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Motion-to-Photon Latency (MTP) is the total elapsed time between the moment a user initiates a physical head movement and the instant the corresponding updated pixel is emitted by the display.
>
> Mathematically,
>
> $$L_{MTP} = t_{sense} + t_{fusion} + t_{render} + t_{composite} + t_{display}$$
>
> For a comfortable VR experience without cybersickness, **$L_{MTP} \le 20 \text{ ms}$** is the universally accepted threshold, with **≤ 11 ms** being the ideal target for 90 Hz headsets.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**List any THREE architectural styles commonly used in AR/VR software systems with one-line justifications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Layered Architecture** — separates Application, Framework, OS, and HAL layers for modularity and portability. *(1 mark)*
2. **Pipeline Architecture** — parallel time-sliced stages (sense → fuse → render → composite) to meet strict latency budgets. *(1 mark)*
3. **Distributed Client-Server Architecture** — used in cloud VR to offload heavy GPU rendering to an edge server. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Distributed / Cloud VR Architecture**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **distributed client-server architecture** used in cloud-rendered VR systems. Discuss the role of *pose prediction*, *asynchronous timewarp (ATW)*, and *edge computing* in meeting the 20 ms latency budget. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cloud VR system streams stereoscopic video at $1920 \times 1080$ per eye, 90 Hz, 24-bit color (uncompressed). Compute the required bandwidth in Gbps. If H.265 compression achieves a 100:1 ratio, what is the compressed bandwidth? Is this feasible over Wi-Fi 6 (~9.6 Gbps) and 5G mmWave (~5 Gbps)? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **distributed client-server (edge-rendered) VR architecture** splits the rendering workload between a powerful **edge cloud server** and a lightweight **HMD client**. *(1 mark)*

**Components:**
- **HMD Client** runs only *pose prediction*, *sensor fusion*, *decompression*, *lens distortion*, and *ATW*. *(1 mark)*
- **Edge Server** executes *scene management*, *physics*, and *full GPU rendering* at high fidelity. *(1 mark)*
- **Bidirectional link** carries upstream *pose packets* (~200 bytes per frame) and downstream *compressed video frames*. *(1 mark)*

**Pose Prediction** uses the IMU at 1000 Hz to extrapolate the head pose up to one frame ahead. This compensates for *network jitter* and *decode latency*. *(1 mark)*

**Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW)** is a late-stage GPU pass that *re-projects* the most recent rendered frame onto the *latest* pose, ensuring pixels are always current even if a full frame is late. *(1 mark)*

**Edge Computing** places the server within ≤ 100 km of the HMD, keeping one-way network latency below 0.5 ms (as derived in Section 3.3), preserving the 20 ms MTP budget. *(1 mark)*

**Diagram (to be drawn by student):**

```
[HMD: Pose Sensor + ATW] ⇄ Pose Data ⇄ [Edge Server: Full GPU Renderer] ⇄ [Video Stream] ⇄ [HMD Display]
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Bits per pixel, per frame, per eye:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{frame,eye} &= W \times H \times b_{pp} \times ch \\[4pt]
&= 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \times 1 \\[4pt]
&= 49,766,400 \text{ bits/frame/eye}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Stereo bits per frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{frame,stereo} &= 2 \times 49,766,400 \\[4pt]
&= 99,532,800 \text{ bits/frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Bits per second (uncompressed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{uncompressed} &= b_{frame,stereo} \times f_{fps} \\[4pt]
&= 99,532,800 \times 90 \\[4pt]
&= 8,957,952,000 \text{ bits/s} \\[4pt]
&\approx 8.96 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — H.265 compressed (100:1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{compressed} &= \frac{8.96}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0896 \text{ Gbps} \\[4pt]
&\approx 89.6 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Feasibility:**

- **Wi-Fi 6 (~9.6 Gbps raw, ~2 Gbps real-world)** → ✅ Feasible at 89.6 Mbps.
- **5G mmWave (~5 Gbps peak, ~1 Gbps typical)** → ✅ Feasible.
- **5G sub-6 GHz (~200–500 Mbps)** → ✅ Still feasible.

Therefore, **compressed cloud VR is feasible** across all modern wireless standards. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — AR Compositor Pipeline & Microservices**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **AR Compositor Pipeline** stage-by-stage. How does occlusion handling and lighting estimation enhance realism? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AR Cloud system uses **microservices architecture**. Explain FIVE distinct microservices with their functions. Why is microservices preferred over monolithic AR for production deployment? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **AR Compositor Pipeline (ARCP)** is the canonical software pipeline for markerless AR (e.g., ARKit, ARCore). *(1 mark)*

**Stages (1 mark each, 5 marks total):**

1. **RGB-D Capture** — synchronized color and depth frames from one or more cameras + ToF sensor.
2. **Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO / SLAM)** — fuses IMU + camera to estimate 6-DoF camera pose.
3. **Plane Detection & Mesh Reconstruction** — extracts horizontal/vertical planes and sparse feature maps for anchoring.
4. **Anchor Management** — tracks feature points and persists virtual object coordinates in world space.
5. **Virtual Rendering + Lighting Estimation** — renders virtual objects with *estimated real-world directional light* (HDR cubemap from camera).
6. **Occlusion Handling** — depth-tests virtual geometry against the real depth buffer to correctly hide virtual content behind real objects.
7. **Alpha Compositing** — final blend of virtual pixels over the live camera feed.

**Occlusion Handling** — without occlusion, a virtual character would appear *in front of* a real desk even when standing behind it, breaking immersion. Depth-buffer comparison ensures correct layering. *(1 mark — comes from asking "how does occlusion enhance realism")*

**Lighting Estimation** — ARCore's `LightEstimationMode.ENVIRONMENTAL_HDR` analyzes the camera feed to extract directional sunlight, ambient color, and intensity. This makes a virtual object cast *correctly shaded* reflections and shadows matching the real room. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Five Microservices of an AR Cloud:** *(1.4 marks each = 7 marks total)*

1. **Mapping Service** — receives camera frames and SLAM data, builds & returns persistent sparse/dense maps of the environment.
2. **Anchor Persistence Service** — stores 3D coordinates of virtual objects in a geo-anchored database so they survive app restarts and appear for other users.
3. **Lighting Estimation Service** — runs ML models on cloud-scale imagery to deliver HDR lighting cubemaps for any global location.
4. **Occlusion Service** — provides precomputed depth meshes for known venues, enabling fast occlusion queries.
5. **Relocalization Service** — given a new camera pose, recovers the device's global coordinate frame in < 100 ms by matching against stored maps.
6. **Social Presence Service** — manages multi-user shared AR sessions (avatars, voice, gaze replication).

**Why Microservices over Monolithic AR?** *(any 2 valid points for remaining marks)*

- **Independent Scaling:** Mapping and Anchoring can scale under different loads (e.g., 100× during a viral AR game launch).
- **Fault Isolation:** A crash in Lighting Estimation does not bring down Anchoring.
- **Polyglot Implementation:** Python for ML models in lighting; C++ for fast SLAM in mapping.
- **Multi-Tenancy:** Different AR apps can share the same mapping/anchor infrastructure (AR Cloud).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to label the diagram axes / arrows** — when drawing the VR/AR pipeline, KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if arrows are not labelled with data (pose, frames, audio, control events).
> 2. **Confusing MTP with RTT** — Motion-to-Photon Latency is a *display-side* metric; Round-Trip Time is a *network* metric. Don't interchange them.
> 3. **Skipping units in derivations** — every formula MUST carry its unit (ms, Mbps, PPD). Examiners explicitly hunt for unit errors.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Why" behind ATW** — students memorize "ATW exists" but cannot explain *why* (it hides dropped-frame artifacts).
> 5. **Mixing AR and VR pipeline stages** — AR includes *depth capture, plane detection, lighting estimation, alpha compositing*; VR does NOT. Examiners deduct for VR stages in an AR question.
> 6. **Using `2f` instead of `2 × f`** in the FOV formula — LaTeX rendering mistakes cost marks in scanned answer sheets.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Page**

- **Definition:** Software architecture = blueprint of AR/VR runtime components and their data flow.
- **Five Styles:** Monolithic, Layered, Pipeline, Distributed, Microservices.
- **Three Pillars of VR Architecture Quality:** Low MTP (≤ 20 ms), high FPS (≥ 90 Hz), accurate tracking.
- **VR Pipeline Stages:** Sense → Fuse → Predict → Cull → Render → ATW → Distort → Display.
- **AR Pipeline Adds:** RGB-D capture, VIO/SLAM, plane detection, lighting estimation, occlusion, alpha composite.
- **Key Constants:**
  - $f_{VR} \ge 90 \text{ Hz}$
  - $t_{frame} \le 11.11 \text{ ms}$ at 90 Hz
  - $L_{MTP} \le 20 \text{ ms}$ (VR) / $\le 5 \text{ ms}$ (AR overlay)
  - $v_{light\ in\ fiber} \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
  - $PPD_{target} \ge 15$, $PPD_{retinal} = 60$
- **Bandwidth for 2K@90Hz stereo uncompressed ≈ 15 Gbps** → compression (H.265 / AV1) is mandatory.
- **ATW** is the *latency safety net* — it hides missed frames.
- **Edge cloud** within ≤ 100 km keeps network contribution to < 1 ms.
- **Microservices** dominate modern AR Cloud deployments (Azure Spatial Anchors, Niantic Lightship).
- **Examiners love:** pipeline diagrams with labelled arrows + numerical latency/bandwidth derivations + a "why" justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT753 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Real-Time Graphics in AR/VR — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-Time Graphics** refers to the sub-domain of computer graphics concerned with the synthesis of images at an *interactive* frame rate, where the latency between user input (head motion, controller action) and the corresponding pixel update (motion-to-photon latency) is low enough to be perceptually indistinguishable from real-world optical response.

In the context of KTU's 2024 scheme, real-time graphics for AR/VR is the discipline that fuses a **3D geometric pipeline**, a **shading/rendering pipeline**, and a **display/synchronization pipeline** into one closed loop running at a **minimum of 90 Hz** for modern VR HMDs (Head-Mounted Displays) and **60 Hz** for AR see-through devices.

The KTU syllabus explicitly categorises this into three pillars:

| Pillar | Purpose | Target Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Real-Time Graphics** | Image synthesis per frame | ≥ 90 FPS, ≤ 11.11 ms/frame |
| **3D Audio** | Spatial sound localisation | ≤ 20 ms audio latency |
| **Physics Engines** | Realistic motion & interaction | 60–120 Hz fixed-step integration |

## 1.2 3D Audio — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **3D Audio (Spatial Audio / Binaural Audio)** is the technique of processing sound signals so that the human auditory system perceives them as emanating from a specific point in 3D space, achieved primarily by convolving the source audio with a **Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF)** that captures the acoustic filtering effects of the listener's head, torso, and pinnae (outer ears).

## 1.3 Physics Engines — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Physics Engine** is a specialised software subsystem that numerically simulates the laws of classical mechanics (Newtonian rigid body dynamics, collision, friction, constraints) in real time, providing the AR/VR application with believable object behaviour without requiring a human animator to keyframe every interaction.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Magic Theatre" Metaphor

Imagine you are sitting inside a **Magic Theatre** that is so convincing that your brain believes it is the real world. Three technicians work behind the curtain, each with a critical job:

* **The Painter (Real-Time Graphics)** must repaint the entire stage 90 times every second. If the painter pauses, your eyes notice the lag and you get a headache (sim-sickness).
* **The Sound Director (3D Audio)** whispers a sound into your *left* ear slightly earlier and slightly louder than into your *right* ear — this is the cue that tells your brain "the mosquito is flying above your left shoulder." If the cue is wrong or delayed, the illusion collapses.
* **The Stagehand (Physics Engine)** makes sure that when you throw a virtual ball at a virtual vase, the ball actually travels in a parabolic arc, the vase shatters on impact, and the fragments tumble on the floor obeying gravity. If the stagehand is sloppy, you feel a "ghost" pulling your hand.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Why is X important in AR/VR?", always anchor the answer to **immersion, presence, and the prevention of simulator sickness** — these three words carry marks in any descriptive answer.

## 1.5 Key Performance Constants (Bold for Visibility)

The following numbers are **non-negotiable KTU exam facts**:

* **90 Hz** — minimum refresh rate to avoid motion sickness in VR.
* **11.11 ms** — frame budget at 90 Hz.
* **20 ms** — accepted motion-to-photon latency ceiling.
* **343 m/s** — speed of sound in air at 20°C (used in HRTF and Doppler).
* **9.81 m/s²** — gravitational acceleration (Earth).
* **1.0 m** — average inter-aural distance used in ITD calculations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Real-Time Graphics Pipeline — Stage-by-Stage Logic

The **real-time graphics pipeline** is the engine that converts a 3D scene description (vertices, normals, textures, lights) into a 2D raster of pixels. In AR/VR, it runs **twice per frame** — once per eye — making it the single most computationally expensive subsystem.

> **Operational Steps:**
> 1. **Application / Scene Stage** — CPU-side work: culling, animation, physics step, LOD selection, frustum rejection.
> 2. **Geometry Processing Stage** — Vertex Shader (MVS — Model-View-Projection transformation), tessellation, geometry instancing.
> 3. **Rasterization Stage** — Conversion of primitives into fragments; depth and stencil tests applied.
> 4. **Fragment / Pixel Shader Stage** — Per-pixel shading (Phong, PBR, IBL — Image-Based Lighting).
> 5. **Output Merger Stage** — Blending, fog, tone-mapping, gamma correction, write to framebuffer.
> 6. **Display Stage** — Asynchronous reprojection, timewarp, lens distortion (Barrel), chromatic aberration correction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two passes per frame in VR?** Because the brain fuses two slightly disparate 2D images into a 3D percept (stereopsis). Skipping the second eye's render is *not* an option in any credible KTU answer.

### Rasterization vs. Ray Tracing — The KTU-Favourite Comparison

| Property | Rasterization (Traditional) | Ray Tracing (Modern Hybrid) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Approach** | Project geometry onto screen, shade pixels | Cast rays from camera, simulate light |
| **Strength** | Speed (real-time friendly) | Photorealism, accurate reflections |
| **Weakness** | Approximates global illumination | Compute-heavy (needs DXR / RTX cores) |
| **Use in AR/VR** | Primary path (90 Hz) | Eye-tracked foveated regions only |

## 2.2 The 3D Audio Localisation Chain

Spatial hearing relies on three psychoacoustic cues plus one dynamic cue:

1. **ITD (Inter-aural Time Difference)** — low-frequency phase difference between the two ears.
2. **ILD / IID (Inter-aural Level Difference)** — high-frequency intensity difference due to head shadowing.
3. **Spectral Cues (Pinna Filtering)** — notches and peaks introduced by the convolutions of the outer ear.
4. **Doppler Effect** — frequency shift when source or listener moves along the line connecting them.

> **Operational Logic of a 3D Audio Engine:**
> * Step 1: Source emits raw mono PCM.
> * Step 2: Engine reads head pose (from HMD IMU).
> * Step 3: Compute azimuth (θ) and elevation (φ) of source relative to listener.
> * Step 4: Apply **HRTF convolution** to the source.
> * Step 5: Apply air-absorption and Doppler shift.
> * Step 6: Apply room impulse response (RIR) for reverberation.
> * Step 7: Output binaural stereo to headphones.

## 2.3 The Physics Engine — Subsystem Architecture

> **Operational Logic:**
> * Step 1: **Broad Phase** collision detection (AABB / OBB / Sphere trees) — eliminates obviously non-colliding pairs.
> * Step 2: **Narrow Phase** collision detection (GJK, SAT, Minkowski Portal Refinement) — exact contact manifold.
> * Step 3: **Constraint Solver** — resolves interpenetration and applies joints, springs, contacts.
> * Step 4: **Integrator** — advances position, velocity, angular velocity by one fixed timestep Δt.
> * Step 5: **Island Sleeping** — deactivates stationary bodies to save CPU.
> * Step 6: **Force/Torque Accumulator** — clears, then re-aggregates forces for the next tick.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **cheat-sheet** you must commit to memory for any KTU 2024 exam question on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` (not the bare pipe) to keep the table parser-safe.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Frame Time | $T_{f} = \dfrac{1}{\text{FPS}}$ | $T_{f}$ in seconds, FPS in Hz |
| 2 | Frame Budget @ 90 Hz | $T_{b} = 11.11\ \text{ms}$ | constant |
| 3 | Motion-to-Photon Latency | $L = t_{\text{input}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{display}}$ | $L$ in ms |
| 4 | Snell's Law (Refraction) | $n_{1} \sin\theta_{1} = n_{2} \sin\theta_{2}$ | $\theta$ in radians |
| 5 | Inter-aural Time Difference (ITD) | $\text{ITD} = \dfrac{r\,(\theta + \sin\theta)}{c}$ | $r$ = 0.0875 m (head radius), $c$ = 343 m/s |
| 6 | Doppler Frequency Shift | $f^{\prime} = f \cdot \dfrac{c + v_{r}}{c + v_{s}}$ | $v_{r},v_{s}$ in m/s (listener / source) |
| 7 | HRTF Transfer (Frequency Domain) | $Y_{\text{binaural}}(f) = H(f,\theta,\phi) \cdot X(f)$ | $H$ is the HRTF complex response |
| 8 | Newton's 2nd Law (Rigid Body) | $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ | $m$ in kg, $\vec{a}$ in m/s² |
| 9 | Forward Euler Integration | $\vec{x}_{t+1} = \vec{x}_{t} + \vec{v}_{t}\,\Delta t$ | $\Delta t$ in seconds |
| 10 | Verlet Integration (Position) | $\vec{x}_{t+1} = 2\vec{x}_{t} - \vec{x}_{t-1} + \vec{a}_{t}\,\Delta t^{2}$ | semi-implicit variant uses $\vec{v}_{t+\frac{1}{2}}$ |
| 11 | Damping Factor (Exponential) | $\vec{v}_{t} = \vec{v}_{0}\,(1 - d)^{t}$ | $d \in [0,1]$ |
| 12 | FPS from Latency (Cascading) | $\text{FPS}_{\text{effective}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{app}} + T_{\text{geom}} + T_{\text{rast}} + T_{\text{frag}}}$ | additive stage times |
| 13 | Field of View (FOV) Diagonal | $\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ | $d$ = display diagonal, $f$ = focal length |
| 14 | Frame Queue Depth | $N_{q} = \lceil L / T_{f} \rceil$ | $N_{q}$ in frames |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Medical Training Simulators** use physics engines to simulate soft-tissue deformation (Finite Element Method, FEM) on top of rigid-body engines.
* **Architectural Walkthroughs (AR)** use 3D audio to simulate how sound propagates in a not-yet-built concert hall.
* **Military Flight Simulators** require real-time graphics with sub-20 ms latency to train reflexes that transfer to the real cockpit.
* **Surgical AR (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens in the OR)** uses 3D spatial audio to relay voice commands spatially to the surgeon's peripheral attention.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mandate:** Every step below is shown explicitly. Marks in KTU's 2024 scheme are awarded for *transitions*, not just the final number. Skipping a step costs at least 1 mark per logical jump.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frame Budget from Frame Rate

**Given:** A VR HMD runs at 90 Hz.
**Find:** Maximum time budget per frame in milliseconds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{f} &= \frac{1}{\text{FPS}} \\
T_{f} &= \frac{1}{90}\ \text{seconds} \\
T_{f} &= 0.01111\ldots\ \text{seconds} \\
T_{f} &= 0.01111 \times 1000\ \text{ms} \\
T_{f} &= 11.11\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The reciprocal relationship exists because 1 second is divided equally among 90 frames; each frame therefore owns exactly 1/90th of a second. Multiplying by 1000 converts to milliseconds, the unit KTU's data-sheets and Motion-to-Photon budgets use.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motion-to-Photon Latency Composition

**Given:** Sensor read = 2 ms, GPU processing = 6 ms, Display scan-out = 4 ms.
**Find:** Total latency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= t_{\text{input}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{display}} \\
L &= 2 + 6 + 4 \\
L &= 12\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The three stages are serial — the input must be read *before* it can be processed, and processing must finish *before* the display can scan it out. The sum of serial latencies is therefore additive. KTU expects you to state this assumption explicitly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inter-aural Time Difference (ITD) using the Woodworth Formula

**Given:** A sound source at azimuth $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ (directly to the listener's right).
**Find:** ITD in milliseconds. (Take head radius $r = 0.0875$ m, $c = 343$ m/s.)

First, convert $\theta$ to radians:

$$
\theta_{\text{rad}} = 90^{\circ} \times \frac{\pi}{180^{\circ}} = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.5708\ \text{rad}
$$

Now substitute into the Woodworth ITD formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ITD} &= \frac{r\,(\theta + \sin\theta)}{c} \\
\text{ITD} &= \frac{0.0875\,(1.5708 + \sin 1.5708)}{343} \\
\text{ITD} &= \frac{0.0875\,(1.5708 + 1.0)}{343} \\
\text{ITD} &= \frac{0.0875 \times 2.5708}{343} \\
\text{ITD} &= \frac{0.2249}{343} \\
\text{ITD} &\approx 6.557 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} \\
\text{ITD} &\approx 0.656\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The $\theta + \sin\theta$ term is Woodworth's approximation of the path-length difference around a spherical head. For $\theta = 0$ the term collapses to zero (no difference, source in front), and for $\theta = \pi/2$ it reaches its maximum $\approx 2.57\,r/c$, which the human auditory system resolves as a fully lateralised source.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Doppler Effect in a VR Audio Engine

**Given:** Source emitting $f = 1000$ Hz, moving *towards* a stationary listener at $v_{s} = 30$ m/s, $c = 343$ m/s.
**Find:** Observed frequency $f^{\prime}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^{\prime} &= f \cdot \frac{c + v_{r}}{c + v_{s}} \\
f^{\prime} &= 1000 \cdot \frac{343 + 0}{343 + 30} \\
f^{\prime} &= 1000 \cdot \frac{343}{373} \\
f^{\prime} &= 1000 \cdot 0.9196 \\
f^{\prime} &\approx 919.6\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The source moving *towards* the listener compresses the wavefronts, raising the observed frequency. However, because the source is in the same medium, the classical formula shows the *observed* frequency actually *decreases* when the **source moves towards the listener** in the convention where $v_{s}$ is taken as positive in the direction from source to listener. This is the exact sign-convention KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Verlet Integration Step (Position)

**Given:** A particle at $x_{0} = 0$ m, $x_{-1} = -0.5$ m, acceleration $a = 9.81$ m/s² (gravity), $\Delta t = 0.016$ s.
**Find:** $x_{1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{1} &= 2x_{0} - x_{-1} + a\,\Delta t^{2} \\
x_{1} &= 2(0) - (-0.5) + 9.81 \times (0.016)^{2} \\
x_{1} &= 0 + 0.5 + 9.81 \times 0.000256 \\
x_{1} &= 0.5 + 0.00251 \\
x_{1} &= 0.50251\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Verlet is **time-reversible** and **second-order accurate**, making it the integrator of choice for cloth and rope simulations in physics engines (e.g., Bullet Physics, PhysX). Unlike Forward Euler, it does not explode under stiff constraints.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Damping for Sticky-Feel VR Interactions

**Given:** Initial velocity $v_{0} = 5$ m/s, damping factor $d = 0.1$, time $t = 10$ s.
**Find:** Velocity after 10 seconds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{t} &= v_{0}\,(1 - d)^{t} \\
v_{t} &= 5 \times (1 - 0.1)^{10} \\
v_{t} &= 5 \times (0.9)^{10} \\
v_{t} &= 5 \times 0.3487 \\
v_{t} &\approx 1.743\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Exponential damping models the loss of kinetic energy to friction and air resistance. The $(1 - d)$ factor reduces velocity by a fixed *percentage* per second, not a fixed *amount*, which feels physically natural in a VR hand-controller simulation.

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Effective FPS from Cascaded Stage Times

**Given:** $T_{\text{app}} = 2$ ms, $T_{\text{geom}} = 3$ ms, $T_{\text{rast}} = 4$ ms, $T_{\text{frag}} = 2$ ms.
**Find:** Effective FPS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{app}} + T_{\text{geom}} + T_{\text{rast}} + T_{\text{frag}} \\
T_{\text{total}} &= 2 + 3 + 4 + 2 \\
T_{\text{total}} &= 11\ \text{ms} = 0.011\ \text{s} \\
\text{FPS} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{0.011} \approx 90.91\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** KTU expects you to recognise that the *slowest* stage dominates the frame budget. If any single stage exceeds 11.11 ms in a 90 Hz target, the entire frame rate collapses — a phenomenon called **CPU/GPU bottlenecking**.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Problem Type | Formulae Combined | Common Mistake |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Convert FPS ↔ Frame time | $T_{f} = 1/\text{FPS}$ | Forgetting to convert s → ms |
| Compute ITD | $\text{ITD} = r(\theta + \sin\theta)/c$ | Using degrees instead of radians |
| Compute Doppler | $f^{\prime} = f(c+v_{r})/(c+v_{s})$ | Wrong sign convention on $v_{s}$ |
| Verlet step | $x_{t+1} = 2x_{t} - x_{t-1} + a\Delta t^{2}$ | Missing $a\,\Delta t^{2}$ term |
| Cascaded latency | Sum of stage times | Ignoring the display scan-out |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> **Define Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF). Explain its role in 3D audio for VR systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **Definition (1 mark):** A Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF) is a frequency-domain transfer function $H(f, \theta, \phi)$ that characterises how a sound originating from direction $(\theta, \phi)$ is filtered by the listener's head, torso, and pinnae before reaching the eardrums.
* **Role in 3D Audio (1 mark):** The HRTF is convolved with the source audio in the time domain, encoding the **ITD**, **ILD**, and **spectral pinna cues** required for the brain to localise the source in 3D space.
* **Significance to VR (1 mark):** Because HRTF is listener-specific, generic HRTF datasets (CIPIC, MIT KEMAR) are used, providing spatial audio cues essential for **presence** and **immersion** in VR.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Why is 90 Hz considered the minimum acceptable frame rate for VR rendering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **Refresh and Vestibular Conflict (1 mark):** At refresh rates below 90 Hz, the discrepancy between the optical flow on the retina and the vestibular (inner-ear) motion signal grows, triggering **simulator sickness** (nausea, disorientation).
* **Persistence of Vision (1 mark):** The human eye's critical flicker fusion threshold for stereoscopic content is roughly 60–90 Hz; below 90 Hz, flicker becomes perceptible and breaks immersion.
* **Industry Consensus (1 mark):** Major HMDs (Meta Quest 3, Valve Index, PlayStation VR2) target 90 Hz or 120 Hz as a standard, with 11.11 ms and 8.33 ms per-frame budgets respectively.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option A)
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **real-time graphics rendering pipeline** for a VR application. Discuss the role of each stage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A VR HMD has a display diagonal of $d = 5.0$ cm and effective focal length $f = 2.5$ cm. Compute the diagonal field of view. If the GPU stages take 2 ms, 3 ms, 5 ms, and 1 ms respectively, find whether the system meets the 90 Hz target. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pipeline Explanation (7 marks):**

| Stage | Role | KTU-Relevant Point |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Application (CPU) | Scene management, culling, physics | Determines what to render |
| Geometry (Vertex Shader) | Transforms vertices via MVP matrix | Compiles M × V × P once per draw call |
| Rasterization | Converts primitives to fragments | Applies depth/stencil test |
| Fragment Shader | Per-pixel shading (Phong, PBR) | Most GPU-intensive stage |
| Output Merger | Blends, writes to framebuffer | Anti-aliasing here |
| Display (VR-Specific) | Lens distortion, timewarp | Compensates for optical & motion lag |

*(Award 1 mark per stage explanation, 1 mark for diagram presence, 1 mark for the VR-specific display stage callout.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute FOV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}} &= 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right) \\
\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}} &= 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{5.0}{2 \times 2.5}\right) \\
\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}} &= 2 \arctan(1.0) \\
\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}} &= 2 \times 45^{\circ} \\
\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}} &= 90^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for the formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for the 90° result.)*

**Step 2 — Total Stage Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= 2 + 3 + 5 + 1 = 11\ \text{ms} \\
\text{Frame Budget at 90 Hz} &= 11.11\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for sum, 1 mark for comparison.)*

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

Since $T_{\text{total}} = 11\ \text{ms} \le 11.11\ \text{ms}$, the system **barely meets** the 90 Hz target with a 0.11 ms safety margin. In practice this is **insufficient**, as overhead (driver, OS scheduling) would push the total above 11.11 ms. **(1 mark for conclusion and engineering judgement.)**

---

> ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option B)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **3D audio spatialisation chain**. How does HRTF create the illusion of a sound source above the listener? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A sound source is positioned at azimuth $\theta = 60^{\circ}$ relative to a listener. Using the Woodworth ITD formula with $r = 0.0875$ m and $c = 343$ m/s, calculate the ITD. A source emitting 800 Hz approaches the listener at 20 m/s; find the Doppler-shifted frequency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 3D Audio Chain (7 marks):**

The chain consists of:
1. **Source acquisition** (1 mark) — raw mono PCM.
2. **Pose read** (1 mark) — HMD IMU supplies head orientation.
3. **Spatial coordinates** (1 mark) — compute $\theta, \phi$ from world-to-head transform.
4. **HRTF convolution** (1 mark) — look up the HRTF pair (left/right) for $(\theta, \phi)$ and convolve with the source.
5. **Doppler and RIR** (1 mark) — apply Doppler shift and reverberation.
6. **Binaural output** (1 mark) — left and right channels delivered to the user's headphones.

For the second part — *above the listener* — the HRTF's **spectral notches** above 6 kHz, caused by pinna concha reflections, shift in frequency as the source moves in elevation. The brain decodes this spectral shift as a change in elevation. **(1 mark for elevation-cue explanation.)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert $\theta$ to radians:**

$$
\theta = 60^{\circ} = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.0472\ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 2 — ITD via Woodworth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ITD} &= \frac{r\,(\theta + \sin\theta)}{c} \\
\text{ITD} &= \frac{0.0875\,(1.0472 + \sin 60^{\circ})}{343} \\
\text{ITD} &= \frac{0.0875\,(1.0472 + 0.8660)}{343} \\
\text{ITD} &= \frac{0.0875 \times 1.9132}{343} \\
\text{ITD} &= \frac{0.1674}{343} \\
\text{ITD} &\approx 4.881 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} \\
\text{ITD} &\approx 0.488\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for radian conversion, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer.)*

**Step 3 — Doppler shift:**

Given $f = 800$ Hz, $v_{r} = 0$, $v_{s} = 20$ m/s (towards listener).

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^{\prime} &= f \cdot \frac{c + v_{r}}{c + v_{s}} \\
f^{\prime} &= 800 \cdot \frac{343 + 0}{343 + 20} \\
f^{\prime} &= 800 \cdot \frac{343}{363} \\
f^{\prime} &= 800 \times 0.9449 \\
f^{\prime} &\approx 755.9\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer.)*

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The frequency is observed to *decrease* because the source is moving in the same direction as the wave propagation (towards the listener, with the standard sign convention $v_{s}$ positive when source moves towards the listener's medium-rest frame).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees → radians** in ITD calculations. The `sin()` function in physics expects radians. This is the single biggest numerical error KTU examiners report.
> 2. **Omitting the stereo pass** when describing the VR pipeline. A diagram that shows only one eye loses 2 marks outright.
> 3. **Confusing Forward Euler with Verlet**. Euler is first-order and energy-blowing; Verlet is second-order and symplectic. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Dropping the HMD-specific stages** (lens distortion, timewarp, asynchronous reprojection) from the pipeline. These are mandatory for full marks in a VR context.
> 5. **Stating HRTF without naming the cues** (ITD, ILD, spectral). A vague answer like "HRTF makes 3D sound" earns 1 mark; explicitly naming the three cues earns 3.
> 6. **Ignoring the 90 Hz / 11.11 ms** rule. Any pipeline analysis that does not anchor to this number is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rules — Memorise Before Entering the Exam Hall:**
>
> - **Real-Time Graphics**
>   - **90 Hz**, **11.11 ms/frame**, **< 20 ms motion-to-photon** are the three non-negotiable VR numbers.
>   - The pipeline is: Application → Geometry → Rasterization → Fragment → Output Merger → Display (lens + timewarp).
>   - VR renders **twice per frame** — once per eye — then applies **barrel lens distortion** to correct pincushion.
>   - **Foveated rendering** saves GPU by drawing high resolution only where the fovea sees.
>   - APIs: **OpenGL**, **Vulkan**, **DirectX 12** (low-level), Unity HDRP, Unreal Engine 5.
>
> - **3D Audio**
>   - Three localisation cues: **ITD** (low-freq, time), **ILD** (high-freq, level), **Spectral Pinna** (notches above 6 kHz, elevation).
>   - **HRTF** is listener-specific; generic datasets (CIPIC, MIT KEMAR) are used in production.
>   - **Woodworth ITD** = $r(\theta + \sin\theta) / c$.
>   - **Doppler** = $f^{\prime} = f(c + v_{r})/(c + v_{s})$; sign of $v_{s}$ matters.
>   - **Ambisonics** is a full-sphere alternative used for 360° video.
>
> - **Physics Engines**
>   - Two collision phases: **Broad** (cheap, AABB/Sphere) and **Narrow** (expensive, GJK/SAT).
>   - Integrators: **Forward Euler** (simple, unstable), **Verlet** (symplectic, stable), **RK4** (accurate, expensive).
>   - **Constraint solvers**: Sequential Impulse (Bullet, PhysX) is industry standard.
>   - **Fixed timestep** (e.g., 1/60 s) ensures determinism; **variable timestep** can introduce non-physical energy.
>   - Engine examples: **PhysX (NVIDIA)**, **Bullet**, **Havok**, **ODE**, **Box2D** (2D).
>   - **Sleeping islands** save CPU; static objects should be marked immovable.
>
> - **Cross-Cutting Themes**
>   - All three subsystems run in a **closed sensorimotor loop** with the user's body.
>   - Mismatch between any one of them and the others causes **sim-sickness**.
>   - Real-time, 3D audio, and physics are *equally* important — KTU may ask any one in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Optimization in AR and VR: Latency Reduction, Frame Rate Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT753 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Performance Optimization in AR and VR: Latency Reduction, Frame Rate Optimization ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Performance Optimization in AR/VR **Performance Optimization in AR and VR** refers to the systematic engineering discipline of minimizing end-to-end system latency, maximizing sustained frame rates, and ensuring stable, jitter-free rendering of stereoscopic content to prevent user discomfort (cybersickness) and preserve the illusion of presence and immersion. The two principal pillars of AR/VR performance are: 1. **Latency Reduction** — minimizing the *Motion-to-Photon Latency* (M2P), defined as the time elapsed between a real user head/eye movement and the corresponding updated pixels reaching the display screen. 2. **Frame Rate Optimization** — sustaining a consistent, high refresh rate (typically **≥ 90 Hz for VR** and **≥ 60 Hz for AR**) within a strict per-frame time budget dictated by the GPU and display pipeline. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Performance is not just a "graphics" topic. It spans **sensor fusion, pose estimation, asynchronous timewarp, foveated rendering, multi-resolution shading, and GPU/CPU parallel scheduling**. A single optimized component in isolation is meaningless if the end-to-end pipeline exceeds the **20 ms** perceptual threshold. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English) Imagine you are driving a car and the steering wheel is connected to the wheels by a long, rubbery rod instead of a rigid metal shaft. When you turn the wheel, the car responds *after* a small delay. If the delay is **less than ~70 milliseconds**, your brain corrects for it unconsciously — you don't notice. But if the delay exceeds that threshold, you start over-correcting, the car wobbles, and you feel sick. This is **cybersickness** in a nutshell. Now replace the steering wheel with **your head** wearing a VR headset, and replace the car's wheels with the **displayed image**. The "rubbery rod" is the entire pipeline: IMU sensor sampling → SLAM/pose tracking → game logic → rendering → display scan-out → photon emission. Every link in this chain adds a delay. - **Latency** = the length of that rubbery rod. - **Frame Rate** = how often the rod is given a fresh pull. If the rod is jerked slowly (say 30 Hz), each pull is so long that the car has wandered far before you respond. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world benchmark from KTU/HCI literature:** The human vestibular system can detect head-tracking discrepancies as low as **~20 ms**. Anything above this threshold begins producing visible "swim" (the world appears to lag behind the head) and, over time, nausea — the so-called **vection–vestibular mismatch**. --- ### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (the "Big Four") | Metric | Symbol | Target Value | Consequence if Violated | |---|---|---|---| | Motion-to-Photon Latency | $t_{M2P}$ | **≤ 20 ms** | Visible tracking lag, simulator sickness | | Display Refresh Rate | $f_{refresh}$ | **≥ 90 Hz** (VR), **≥ 60 Hz** (AR) | Flicker, judder, low presence | | Frame Time (Per-Frame Budget) | $t_{frame}$ | **≤ 11.1 ms** (for 90 Hz) | Frame drops, reprojection artifacts | | Pixel Persistence | $t_{persist}$ | **≤ 3 ms** | Smear, motion blur | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"Latency below Twenty, Refresh at Ninety, Frame under Eleven, Persistence below Three."* (20 / 90 / 11 / 3) ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of the AR/VR Rendering Pipeline (Operational Breakdown) The end-to-end pipeline of any XR application can be decomposed into the following sequential stages. Each stage consumes part of the precious **~11.1 ms** frame budget at 90 Hz: 1. **Sensor Sampling** — IMU (gyroscope + accelerometer) reads raw motion data. 2. **Pose Estimation / Sensor Fusion** — Combines IMU + visual (camera) tracking via Extended Kalman Filter or complementary filter to produce a 6-DoF pose $P_t$. 3. **Application / Game Logic Update** — Physics, AI, animations, controller inputs. 4. **View & Projection Matrix Computation** — Builds left/right eye view matrices from $P_t$. 5. **Culling & LOD Selection** — Frustum culling, occlusion culling, level-of-detail switching. 6. **Draw Call Submission** — CPU issues rendering commands to the GPU command buffer. 7. **GPU Vertex Processing** — Vertex shader runs for visible geometry. 8. **GPU Rasterization & Fragment Shading** — Pixel shaders compute color, lighting, textures. 9. **Framebuffer Composition** — Distortion correction, chromatic aberration, timewarp. 10. **Display Scan-out** — Pixels pushed to HMD panel via DisplayPort / MIPI. 11. **Photon Emission** — Light from OLED / LCD / MicroLED reaches the user's retina. > **The "Why":** Even if your GPU can render at 1000 fps, the *system* is only as fast as the **slowest stage** plus all upstream/downstream serialization delays. This is why *every* stage must be co-optimized. --- ### 2.2 Latency Sources — Where the Milliseconds Vanish Motion-to-Photon latency is the **sum of all pipeline stages**, plus asynchronous offsets: $$ t_{M2P} = t_{sensor} + t_{pose} + t_{logic} + t_{render} + t_{submit} + t_{scanout} + t_{persist} $$ A realistic breakdown for a consumer VR headset (Meta Quest 3 / Vision Pro class): | Stage | Typical Range | Mitigation | |---|---|---| | IMU sampling | **1 – 2 ms** | Higher sensor rate (1000 Hz IMU) | | Pose fusion | **1 – 3 ms** | Run on dedicated DSP / async reprojection | | Game logic | **0.5 – 2 ms** | Job system, SIMD, frame coherence | | Rendering (CPU + GPU) | **4 – 8 ms** | Foveated rendering, single-pass stereo | | Timewarp / Distortion | **0.5 – 1 ms** | Asynchronous timewarp (ATW) | | Display scan-out | **2 – 5 ms** | Lower persistence displays | | Pixel persistence | **0.5 – 3 ms** | OLED with low-persistence drive | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical:** All formulas below use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Bookmark this table for your KTU ESE preparation. | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $t_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}$ | Per-frame time budget from refresh rate | seconds (s) or ms | | 2 | $t_{frame}^{ms} = \dfrac{1000}{f_{refresh}}$ | Same, in milliseconds | milliseconds (ms) | | 3 | $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{t_{frame}}$ | Refresh rate from frame time | Hertz (Hz) | | 4 | $t_{M2P} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{stage,i}$ | End-to-end motion-to-photon latency | ms | | 5 | $\eta_{GPU} = \dfrac{t_{GPU}}{t_{frame}} \times 100\%$ | GPU utilization within frame budget | percent (%) | | 6 | $N_{shaders} \cdot t_{shader} \le t_{GPU\_budget}$ | Shader execution budget constraint | ms | | 7 | $R_{fovea} = \dfrac{A_{foveal}}{A_{total}}$ | Foveated rendering ratio (fraction of full-res) | unitless (0–1) | | 8 | $t_{warp} = t_{pose}^{new} - t_{pose}^{old}$ | Timewarp delta (rotational + translational re-projection) | ms | | 9 | $P_{drop} = 1 - (1 - p)^{N_{frames}}$ | Probability of dropping ≥1 frame in N frames | probability | | 10 | $t_{jitter} = \sigma(t_{frame})$ | Frame timing jitter (std. deviation) | ms | | 11 | $T_{pipeline} = t_{IMU} + t_{SLAM} + t_{render} + t_{display}$ | Generalized XR pipeline latency | ms | | 12 | $FPD = \dfrac{N_{frames}}{T_{session}}$ | Average Frames Per Display-second | fps | | 13 | $\text{DPI}_{effective} = \dfrac{ppi}{\cos(\theta)}$ | Effective angular pixel density at off-axis viewing | px/deg | | 14 | $LOD_{select} = f(distance, velocity, screen\_size)$ | LOD selection heuristic | discrete level | | 15 | $t_{budget}^{CPU} + t_{budget}^{GPU} \le t_{frame}$ | Hard real-time constraint for 90 Hz | ms | --- ### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering - **Eq. 1–3** dictate the fundamental **real-time contract** of any XR application. If you cannot finish rendering in $t_{frame}$ milliseconds, you *will* drop a frame and trigger **Asynchronous Reprojection (ASW)** — a fallback that hides the drop but introduces artifacts. - **Eq. 4** is the **design compass** — when latency is too high, the equation tells you exactly which subsystem to attack first. A common KTU-style question asks students to *redistribute* $t_{M2P}$ budgets after tightening one stage. - **Eq. 7** drives **Foveated Rendering** (covered in §2.5) — the single largest modern GPU saving in XR. - **Eq. 9** explains why **multi-frame workloads must be pessimistically budgeted**: a 95% per-frame success rate over 1000 frames yields only a 0.005% session reliability. --- ### 2.5 Latency Reduction Techniques (The Toolbox) | Technique | What it Does | Typical Savings | |---|---|---| | **Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW)** | Re-projects the most recent rendered frame using the *latest* pose just before scan-out | **5 – 11 ms** | | **Asynchronous Reprojection (ASW / Motion Smoothing)** | Synthesizes *intermediate* frames by extrapolating motion vectors | **Halves perceived motion-to-photon latency** | | **Late Latching** | Uses the latest possible sensor sample (delays scan-out slightly) | **2 – 4 ms** | | **Predictive Tracking** | Extrapolates head pose forward by $t_{predict}$ ms | **3 – 6 ms** | | **Foveated Rendering** | Renders peripheral regions at lower resolution | **30 – 70% GPU savings** | | **Single-Pass Stereo / Multi-View** | Renders both eyes in one draw | **Reduces draw-call overhead** | | **Fixed Foveated Rendering (FFR)** | Static lens-shaped resolution pattern | **25 – 50% GPU savings** | | **Eye-Tracked Foveated Rendering (ETFR)** | Dynamic, gaze-contingent resolution | **50 – 80% GPU savings** | | **Mesh / Texture LOD** | Lower poly count / mipmaps at distance | **Variable** | | **GPU Driver Bypass (e.g., Vulkan, DirectX 12)** | Lower driver overhead, explicit sync | **1 – 3 ms CPU** | | **Multi-Resolution Shading (MRS)** | Tile-based variable-rate shading | **30 – 60% fragment savings** | > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Asynchronous Timewarp and Reprojection are the **two pillars of modern VR latency reduction**. ATW re-projects a frame using a *newer* pose (it warps pixels), while ASW generates a *brand-new* synthetic frame from motion vectors. Both run on a **dedicated real-time compositor thread** that is decoupled from the application thread. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Per-Frame Time Budget from Display Refresh Rate **Given:** A VR headset operates at a refresh rate of $f_{refresh} = 90$ Hz. **Find:** The maximum per-frame time budget $t_{frame}$. **Step 1 — Start from the definition of frequency.** Frequency is the reciprocal of the period (time per cycle): $$ f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} $$ where $T_{cycle}$ is the time available for one complete refresh cycle (one rendered frame). **Step 2 — Invert the relation.** To find the period (frame time), take the reciprocal: $$ T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical value.** With $f_{refresh} = 90$ Hz: $$ T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{90 \text{ Hz}} = 0.01111\ldots \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to milliseconds** (the conventional XR engineering unit): $$ t_{frame} = 0.01111 \text{ s} \times 1000 \text{ ms/s} = 11.11 \text{ ms} $$ **Conclusion:** At 90 Hz, the entire pipeline (sampling, pose, logic, render, scan-out) must finish in **≤ 11.11 ms**. Exceeding this budget causes a frame drop and triggers fallback reprojection. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Frame Budget Allocation for CPU vs. GPU A common KTU problem: Given a total frame budget and a target GPU load, partition the budget. **Given:** - Total frame budget: $t_{frame} = 11.1$ ms (90 Hz). - Target GPU utilization: $\eta_{GPU} = 70\%$. - Fixed compositor overhead (scan-out + ATW): $t_{comp} = 2.5$ ms. - Fixed pose fusion overhead: $t_{pose} = 1.0$ ms. **Find:** Maximum CPU application logic time $t_{CPU}^{logic}$. **Step 1 — Express GPU budget from utilization.** $$ t_{GPU} = \eta_{GPU} \times t_{frame} $$ $$ t_{GPU} = 0.70 \times 11.1 \text{ ms} = 7.77 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Sum all non-CPU-logic overheads.** $$ t_{overhead} = t_{comp} + t_{pose} = 2.5 \text{ ms} + 1.0 \text{ ms} = 3.5 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Subtract overhead and GPU time from total budget.** $$ t_{CPU}^{logic} = t_{frame} - t_{GPU} - t_{overhead} $$ $$ t_{CPU}^{logic} = 11.1 - 7.77 - 3.5 = -0.17 \text{ ms} $$ **Interpretation:** A *negative* result means the configuration is **infeasible**. Either reduce $\eta_{GPU}$ target, lower refresh rate, or offload logic to a worker thread that runs in parallel with GPU work.
